# React

## Theory

React \- JavaScript library for building UI.

* Several components (.jsx); (can be reused multiple times- eg \<Food /\>\<Food /\> renders independently.)  
* JavaScript XML. HTML-like syntax inside JavaScript.  
* Reusable JavaScript functions.  
* Return JSX (HTML-like code inside JS).  
* Combine JS \+ UI structure.  
* JSX must be transpiled(Compiled into a different form of the same language) by Babel into regular JavaScript.  
  Example:  
  \<h1\>Hello\</h1\>  
  It is actually converted (by Babel) into:  
  React.createElement("h1", null, "Hello");

DOM

* DOM (Document Object Model): browser API that represents the structure and content of a web page as a tree of objects and nodes  
* Real DOM: Actual browser-rendered structure.  
* Direct updates to Real DOM are slow and expensive (entire UI may re-render).

Virtual DOM

* Lightweight JavaScript copy of the Real DOM.  
* React creates a new Virtual DOM after every state change.(When a component's state or props change, indicating a need for a UI update, React is triggered to re-evaluate the component.)  
* Virtual DOM Re-render: A new VDOM tree is created to reflect the updated state, without affecting the actual webpage yet.  
* Diffing: Compares previous vs new Virtual DOM. \-(identifies the minimal set of changes required.)  
* Reconciliation & Batching: Based on diff- calc the most efficient way to update the real DOM. Multiple changes are batched together- a single update cycle- minimise browser reflows and repaints: reconciliation.

Node.js

* Node.js: JavaScript runtime environment.  
* JS runs outside the browser.  
* Provides npm (Node Package Manager) to install libraries and dependencies.  
* Also enables use of JS for frontend and backend development by leveraging the Node.js runtime environment. (full-stack JavaScript development)  
* interact with databases, handle requests, manage server-side operations

Cascading Style Sheets

* .css- formatting for .jsx

## Setup

Installed Node.js → got npm.  
Created project using:  
	npm create vite@latest  
Framework: React  
Variant: JavaScript \+ React Compiler  
Also used:  
npm install  
npm run dev(used if closed proj)

Issue:  
npm run dev outside the project folder but inside main folder: error.(so used cd even in vs while folder was open)

## Creating Components (.jsx)/ Test App

![][image1]![][image2]  
![][image3]![][image4]

## Styling

1) Regular CSS (Global): Simple to use, (used in reference video)  
* naming conflicts in larger projects due to global scoping.  
* Use className, not class

App.css  
import "./App.css";  
function App() {  
  return \<h1 className="title"\>Hello\</h1\>;  
}

2) Inline   
* JavaScript objects with camelCase properties(no space or delimiter).   
* Doesn't support features like hover effects or media queries (device adaptability);   
* Not scalable for complex styles.

\<h1 style={{ color: "red", fontSize: "20px" }}\>  
Hello  
\</h1\>

3) CSS (Scoped)  
* Imports CSS as a module   
* Automatically generate unique, locally scoped class names,   
* prevents conflicts;   
* ideal for medium to large projects.

App.module.css  
import styles from "./App.module.css";  
\<h1 className={styles.title}\>Hello\</h1\>

4) Package  
   Styled Components library writes CSS directly within JavaScript files  
   dynamic, component-scoped styles.  
   To use, install via a package manager. 

## List Rendering

* Basic  
  * Using map(): Transforms array to new array of JSX elements.

function List() {  
  const fruits \= \["Apple", "Orange", "Banana", "Coconut", "Pineapple"\];  
  return (  
    \<ul\>  
      {fruits.map(fruit \=\> \<li key={fruit}\>{fruit}\</li\>)}  
    \</ul\>  
  );  
}  
export default List;

### Overview (React):

* Setup with Vite  
* Understanding JSX compilation  
* Import/export system  
* One parent rule  
* {} for JS inside JSX  
* Syntax variations  
* Image importing  
* Styling with css(local and global scope) and inline (only theoretical for external packages)  
* List rendering
